text:
Arrays are a collection of items stored at contiguous memory locations.
        The idea is to store multiple items of the same type together.

        Key Characteristics:
        - Fixed size in most languages
        - Random access: O(1) time complexity
        - Sequential storage: elements stored contiguously
        - Indexed: each element has an index starting from 0

        Time Complexity:
        - Access: O(1)
        - Search: O(n)
        - Insertion: O(n)
        - Deletion: O(n)

        Space Complexity: O(n)

        Advantages:
        - Fast access to elements using index
        - Memory efficient
        - Cache friendly due to contiguous memory

        Disadvantages:
        - Fixed size (in most languages)
        - Costly insertion/deletion operations
        - Wastes memory if not fully utilized

A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes.
        Each node contains a value and a reference (link) to the next node.

        Types of Linked Lists:
        1. Singly Linked List: Each node has one link (next)
        2. Doubly Linked List: Each node has two links (next and previous)
        3. Circular Linked List: Last node points to first node

        Key Operations:
        - Access: O(n) - must traverse from head
        - Search: O(n) - linear search required
        - Insertion: O(1) - if position is known
        - Deletion: O(1) - if node is known

        Space Complexity: O(n)

Stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure.
        Last element added is the first one to be removed.

        Main Operations:
        - Push: Add element to top - O(1)
        - Pop: Remove from top - O(1)
        - Peek/Top: View top element - O(1)
        - isEmpty: Check if empty - O(1)

        Real-world Applications:
        - Browser back button
        - Undo/Redo functionality
        - Function call stack in programming
        - Depth-First Search (DFS)

Queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure.
        First element added is the first one to be removed.

        Main Operations:
        - Enqueue: Add to rear - O(1)
        - Dequeue: Remove from front - O(1)
        - Front: View first element - O(1)
        - isEmpty: Check if empty - O(1)

        Types: Simple Queue, Circular Queue, Priority Queue, Deque

        Real-world Applications:
        - CPU task scheduling
        - Breadth-First Search (BFS)
        - Message queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka)

Graph is a non-linear data structure consisting of vertices (nodes) and edges.

        Graph Types:
        1. Directed / Undirected
        2. Weighted / Unweighted
        3. Cyclic / Acyclic (DAG)

        Representations:
        1. Adjacency Matrix — Space O(V²), Edge lookup O(1)
        2. Adjacency List  — Space O(V+E), Good for sparse graphs

        Key Algorithms:
        - BFS: O(V+E), shortest path in unweighted graph
        - DFS: O(V+E), topological sort, cycle detection
        - Dijkstra: O((V+E) log V), shortest path with weights
        - Kruskal: O(E log V), minimum spanning tree

Heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies heap property.
        Max Heap: Parent >= Children | Min Heap: Parent <= Children

        Core Operations:
        - Insert      : O(log n)
        - Extract Max : O(log n)
        - Get Max/Min : O(1)
        - Build Heap  : O(n)

        Applications:
        - Priority queues
        - Dijkstra's shortest path
        - Heap sort
        - K-largest/smallest elements

Trie is a tree-like data structure for efficient string storage and retrieval.
        Also called Prefix Tree. Each node represents a character.

        Operations:
        - Insert       : O(m) — m = word length
        - Search       : O(m)
        - Prefix Search: O(m)
        - Delete       : O(m)

        Applications:
        - Autocomplete (Google, keyboards)
        - Spell checkers
        - IP routing tables
        - Dictionary implementations

Overview
An array is the most fundamental and widely-used data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations. Arrays provide constant-time access to elements through indexing and serve as the foundation for many other complex data structures[1].
Characteristics
Arrays have a fixed size determined at the time of creation, and all elements must be of the same data type. Memory allocation is contiguous, meaning elements are stored in adjacent memory locations, which allows for efficient access through index calculation. The first element is typically accessed at index 0 in most programming languages.
Operations and Time Complexity
Access: O(1) - Direct access to any element using its index
Search: O(n) for unsorted arrays, O(log n) for sorted arrays using binary search
Insertion: O(n) in the worst case, as elements may need to be shifted
Deletion: O(n) in the worst case, requiring element shifting to maintain continuity[2]
Advantages
Arrays provide extremely fast access time with O(1) complexity for indexed operations. They offer simple implementation with minimal overhead and efficient memory utilization due to contiguous storage. Cache locality is excellent because adjacent elements are stored together in memory, improving CPU cache performance.
Disadvantages
The fixed size limitation means arrays cannot grow dynamically during runtime. Insertion and deletion operations are expensive, especially in the middle of the array, requiring O(n) time. Memory wastage can occur if the allocated size exceeds actual usage. Additionally, arrays cannot efficiently handle different data types in a single structure.
Real-World Applications
Arrays are extensively used in implementing other data structures like stacks, queues, and hash tables. They serve as the backbone for database management systems for storing records and implementing lookup tables. Image processing applications use multi-dimensional arrays to represent pixels. Mathematical computations, matrix operations, and implementing dynamic programming algorithms all rely heavily on arrays[3].
Implementation Example (C)
int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
int element = arr[2]; // Access: Returns 30

Overview
A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure in which each node has at most two children, referred to as the left child and right child. Binary trees serve as the foundation for many advanced tree structures[1].
Types of Binary Trees
Full Binary Tree: Every node has either 0 or 2 children.
Complete Binary Tree: All levels are fully filled except possibly the last level, which is filled from left to right.
Perfect Binary Tree: All internal nodes have two children and all leaves are at the same level.
Balanced Binary Tree: The height difference between left and right subtrees is at most 1 for every node.
Tree Traversal Methods
In-order (Left-Root-Right): Produces sorted output for Binary Search Trees.
Pre-order (Root-Left-Right): Used for creating a copy of the tree.
Post-order (Left-Right-Root): Used for deleting the tree.
Level-order (Breadth-first): Processes nodes level by level.
Time Complexity
General binary tree operations depend on tree balance:
Search: O(n) worst case, O(log n) for balanced trees
Insertion: O(n) worst case, O(log n) for balanced trees
Deletion: O(n) worst case, O(log n) for balanced trees[2]
Advantages
Hierarchical structure naturally represents relationships. Efficient searching in balanced binary search trees. Provides multiple traversal methods for different use cases. Forms the basis for more advanced tree structures.
Disadvantages
Performance degrades to O(n) in unbalanced trees. More complex implementation compared to linear structures. Requires additional memory for storing child pointers. Balancing mechanisms needed to maintain efficiency.
Real-World Applications
Binary trees are used in file system directory structures and organizational hierarchies. Decision trees in machine learning and AI use binary tree structures. Expression trees represent mathematical and logical expressions. Huffman coding for data compression, XML/HTML DOM representation, and implementing priority queues (using binary heaps) all rely on binary tree concepts[3].

Overview
An AVL tree (named after inventors Adelson-Velsky and Landis) is a self-balancing Binary Search Tree where the heights of left and right subtrees of every node differ by at most 1. AVL trees guarantee O(log n) time complexity for all operations[1].
Balance Factor
The balance factor of a node is calculated as: height(left subtree) - height(right subtree). In AVL trees, the balance factor must be -1, 0, or +1 for every node. If this property is violated, rotations are performed to restore balance.
Rotation Operations
Single Right Rotation (LL): Applied when left subtree of left child causes imbalance.
Single Left Rotation (RR): Applied when right subtree of right child causes imbalance.
Left-Right Rotation (LR): First left rotation on left child, then right rotation on node.
Right-Left Rotation (RL): First right rotation on right child, then left rotation on node.
Time Complexity
All operations guaranteed:
Search: O(log n)
Insertion: O(log n)
Deletion: O(log n)
Height balancing: O(log n)[2]
Advantages
Guaranteed O(log n) performance for all operations. Strictly balanced structure maintains efficiency. Height is always logarithmic relative to number of nodes. Predictable performance for time-critical applications.
Disadvantages
More complex implementation due to rotation logic. Extra storage required for maintaining balance factors or heights. Frequent rotations during insertions and deletions add overhead. More computationally expensive than unbalanced BST for modifications.
Real-World Applications
AVL trees are ideal for applications requiring frequent lookups with fewer modifications. In-memory databases and file systems use AVL trees for indexing. Real-time systems where predictable performance is critical. Network routing tables, game development for collision detection, and implementing ordered sets/maps in standard libraries utilize AVL tree structures[3].

Overview
A heap is a specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property. In a max-heap, parent nodes are greater than or equal to their children. In a min-heap, parent nodes are less than or equal to their children. Heaps are typically implemented as complete binary trees using arrays[1].
Types of Heaps
Max-Heap: The key at the root is the maximum among all keys. Used for implementing priority queues where highest priority is served first.
Min-Heap: The key at the root is the minimum among all keys. Used when lowest priority should be served first.
Operations and Time Complexity
Insertion (Heapify-up): O(log n) - Add element at end and bubble up
Extract Max/Min: O(log n) - Remove root and heapify down
Get Max/Min: O(1) - Root element is always the maximum/minimum
Build Heap: O(n) - Convert array to heap structure[2]

Overview
A graph is a non-linear data structure consisting of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between nodes). Graphs are among the most versatile data structures, capable of representing complex relationships and networks[1].
Types of Graphs
Directed Graph (Digraph): Edges have direction, going from one vertex to another.
Undirected Graph: Edges are bidirectional, with no specific direction.
Weighted Graph: Edges have associated weights or costs.
Unweighted Graph: All edges are treated equally.
Cyclic Graph: Contains at least one cycle (path starting and ending at same vertex).
Acyclic Graph: Contains no cycles (DAG - Directed Acyclic Graph).
